retrieved_docs.title: Inferior Alveolar Nerve Block -- Complications -- Local Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Trismus: Limited mandibular opening may develop due to trauma or inflammation of the medial pterygoid muscle or surrounding tissues. Although often self-limiting, persistent cases may require anti-inflammatory treatment and physical therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthesia Techniques in Dentistry and Oral Surgery -- Complications -- Needle Fracture
retrieved_docs.content: If a needle breaks, the broken fragment should be removed as soon as possible. Broken needle fragments following a mandibular block most commonly sit within the pterygomandibular region. However, migration to the external auditory canal, superficial post-auricular region, and base of the skull has been reported. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthesia Techniques in Dentistry and Oral Surgery -- Complications -- Needle Fracture
retrieved_docs.content: Needle breakage is most likely with mandibular nerve blocks and if a needle with a small diameter is used. Therefore, a 27 gauge or less needle should be used for mandibular blocks. A long needle must be used so that at least 5 mm of the needle is outside the mucosa to allow retrieval in the case of breakage. The mandible should be held firmly, and the patient should be advised against sudden movements. Furthermore, forceful or repeated pre-bending of the needle should be avoided, and the needle should be changed if repeated injections are required.

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthesia Techniques in Dentistry and Oral Surgery -- Complications -- Failure to Achieve Anesthesia
retrieved_docs.content: In addition, the buccal and great auricular nerves can provide accessory innervation to mandibular teeth via the retromolar foramen when present. [11] Injecting a small amount of anesthetic into the retromolar area can help anesthetize any accessory nerves entering the retromolar foramina. Furthermore, contralateral incisive nerve innervation is common, and when profound anesthesia in anterior teeth is not achieved, a contralateral incisive nerve block may be required. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mental Nerve -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Surgeons should know the mental nerve sensory territory and its foramen to perform a successful nerve block and prevent complications during surgical procedures. For example, in plastic surgery, the mental nerve is blocked during chin reconstructive interventions. As a result, in mentoplasties and other chin surgeries, the patient may be awake — the nerve block aids in safe and successful surgery with reduced potential complications. The surgeon may also block the mental nerve in the jaw and lower lip surgeries [12] [13] .

retrieved_docs.title: Local and Regional Anesthesia in Ophthalmology and Ocular Trauma -- Technique or Treatment -- Facial Nerve Blocks
retrieved_docs.content: The O’Brien block achieves temporary palsy of the orbicularis oculi muscle at the facial nerve trunk. The original version of this block is completed proximally at the level of the mandible near the condyloid process. The modified O’Brien block uses a posterior and inferior position along the most superior palpable aspect of the mandible ramus, approximately 3 cm above the mandible angle. This modified technique provides a highly effective nerve block and lid akinesia at a site closer to the stylomastoid foramen. [12] [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Inferior Alveolar Nerve Block -- Complications -- Local Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Soft tissue injury: Postoperative biting of the lip, cheek, or tongue can occur due to residual anesthesia, especially in pediatric or special needs patients. Educating caregivers is essential to prevent self-inflicted trauma.

retrieved_docs.title: Inferior Alveolar Nerve Block -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Proper technique, thorough anatomical knowledge, careful patient assessment, and strict adherence to infection control protocols are essential for minimizing complications. Early recognition and timely management are critical to ensuring optimal patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lingual Nerve -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: An increase in the number of second molar implants has also increased the risk of injury to the nerve [3] . However, the lingual nerve most commonly gets damaged during inferior alveolar nerve block injections and third molar tooth removals [3] [10] . Reports indicate that damage to this nerve occurs during the removal of the third molar between 0 and 23% of the time [11] [12] . The wide variation in reported frequencies of this injury may be in part due to the variations seen in the course of the lingual nerve. Potential mechanisms for preventing lingual nerve injury during third molar surgery include avoiding lingual flap elevation and conducting tooth sectioning [8] [13] .

retrieved_docs.title: Inferior Alveolar Nerve Block -- Complications -- Local Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Pain during injection: Pain can result from rapid deposition, contact with the periosteum, or excessive tissue distention. Proper technique and topical anesthetics can help minimize discomfort.

retrieved_docs.title: Inferior Alveolar Nerve Block -- Complications -- Local Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Hematoma formation: Accidental injury to the pterygoid venous plexus or inferior alveolar artery may lead to hematoma formation, typically presenting as swelling, bruising, and mild discomfort. Proper aspiration and slow injection help minimize this risk. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthesia Techniques in Dentistry and Oral Surgery -- Complications -- Failure to Achieve Anesthesia
retrieved_docs.content: Accessory innervation is a common cause of anesthetic failure and should be suspected when an IANB provides anesthesia for the lower lip but not the dentition. [11] The mylohyoid nerve can provide accessory innervation to mandibular teeth, and an IANB generally fails to anesthetize the mylohyoid nerve as this nerve branches off superior to the point of injection. Furthermore, the sphenomandibular ligament provides a physical barrier to the diffusion of the anesthetic solution to the mylohyoid nerve, and the mylohyoid nerve sometimes travels within the bone.

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthesia Techniques in Dentistry and Oral Surgery -- Complications -- Nerve Injury and Altered Sensation
retrieved_docs.content: The nerve at most significant risk of injury during an IANB is the lingual nerve, as it sits close to the mucosa without the bony protection of the lingula or sulcus colli bone. [9] [22] Nerve injury with maxillary infiltration is very rare. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Local and Regional Anesthesia in Ophthalmology and Ocular Trauma -- Technique or Treatment -- Facial Nerve Blocks
retrieved_docs.content: Either form of this technique leads to akinesia of the facial/temporal and zygomatic branches of the facial nerve that supplies the motor function to the orbicularis oculi. [32] Although the O’Brien block achieves a more akinetic effect than its counterparts, it carries risks of unilateral facial nerve paresis and nerve root injury. Due to the proximity of the glossopharyngeal and vagus nerves, the internal carotid artery, internal jugular vein, and parotid gland, injection sites closer to the stylomastoid foramen in the modified O’Brien version has been implicated in severe complications such as respiratory and vocal cord paralysis. [33] [32] Atkinson Technique

retrieved_docs.title: Inferior Alveolar Nerve Block -- Complications -- Local Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Needle breakage: Although rare with modern single-use needles, breakage can occur, particularly if the needle is inserted up to the hub or if the patient moves suddenly during injection. Retrieval may require surgical intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Inferior Alveolar Nerve Block -- Contraindications -- Relative Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Bleeding disorders or anticoagulant therapy: Although not an absolute contraindication, patients with coagulopathies or those receiving anticoagulant medications have an increased risk of hematoma formation, particularly due to the proximity of the pterygoid venous plexus. Severe trismus or limited mandibular opening: Restricted access to the injection site may hinder accurate needle placement, increasing the risk of technical failure or iatrogenic injury. Psychological or behavioral conditions (eg, severe dental anxiety and uncooperative pediatric patients): Such cases may require alternative approaches, including sedation or general anesthesia, to ensure patient safety and cooperation during the procedure. Neurological disorders involving the mandibular nerve: Preexisting conditions such as trigeminal neuralgia or prior nerve injury require careful risk-benefit evaluation and consideration of alternative anesthetic strategies when appropriate.

Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

This framework supports precise anatomical localization, differential diagnosis, and therapeutic planning, especially the central role of nerve decompression procedures across multiple anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2241
retrieved_docs.content: Incision of nerve is classified as a type of peripheral nerve decompression, indicating that it falls under the broader category of decompression procedures targeting peripheral nerves.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2482
retrieved_docs.content: جراحة (surgery) serves as a broad category that includes procedures such as ultrazvuková chirurgie (ultrasound‑guided surgery) and البزل (aspiration), both of which are classified as subtypes under the parent concept of جراحة. Within the domain of جراحة, عمليات الأنف (nose surgeries) are clinically associated with جراحة, indicating that nasal operative interventions are a specific application of general surgical practice. Diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx are organized as a subcategory of عمليات الأنف, reflecting that examinations of the nasal, oral, and pharyngeal regions are considered part of the broader group of nasal surgeries. A notable postoperative complication, postoperative complete heart block, can occur after جراحة, highlighting the need for cardiac monitoring following surgical interventions. Understanding these hierarchical relationships among general surgery, its specialized forms, and associated diagnostic and postoperative considerations aids clinicians in organizing care pathways and anticipating potential adverse events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 1274
retrieved_docs.content: organized under nervous system and sense organ diseases, which themselves are subsumed by the overarching concept of pain. Specific nerve injuries and peripheral neuropathy are linked to pain, and pain is further connected to complex syndromes such as phantom limb syndrome, underscoring the central role of pain in the clinical understanding and management of these interconnected medical entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2497
retrieved_docs.content: OPERATIONS AND ANESTHESIA PROCEDURES is categorized under منظار داخلي (internal endoscopy), indicating that endoscopic techniques are considered a parent framework for many operative and anesthetic interventions. The Arabic term جراحة (surgery) is also linked to منظار داخلي as a sub‑category, reflecting the close association between surgical practice and endoscopic methods. Diagnostic procedures on nose, mouth and pharynx are specifically related to منظار داخلي, emphasizing that endoscopic examination is a key tool for evaluating these upper‑airway regions, and these diagnostic procedures are further classified under عمليات الأنف (nose surgeries), showing that they belong to the broader surgical domain of nasal procedures. Clinically, جراحة is associated with عمليات الأنف, underscoring that nasal surgeries are a distinct subset of general surgical care. Within the surgical hierarchy, ultrazvuková chirurgie (ultrasound‑guided surgery) is a child of جراحة, illustrating that advanced imaging‑assisted techniques are nested under the general surgery category. Finally, Postoperative complete heart block is noted to occur after جراحة, highlighting that this serious cardiac conduction complication can arise as a postoperative sequela of any surgical operation, including those performed endoscopically or with ultrasound guidance. Together, these relationships depict a structured network in which endoscopic methods, general and specialized surgeries, and their diagnostic and postoperative considerations are interlinked for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1694
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is classified as تروية دموية and is a subcategory of other or procedures on vessels of head and neck, indicating that interventions directed at blood vessels are considered vascularization procedures and belong to the broader group of head and neck vessel procedures.

retrieved_docs.content: these interconnections demonstrate how peripheral‑nerve pathology, entrapment‑syndrome decompression, pain and migraine mechanisms, and musculoskeletal disorders converge in neurological and musculoskeletal patient care, guiding diagnosis, therapeutic planning, and preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 86
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base groups a wide array of neurologic and neuromuscular conditions under the umbrella of **Other disorders of the nervous system**, emphasizing their inter‑relationships with pain, paralysis, and sensory disturbance. Generic pain classifications (including “Pain, not elsewhere classified”) and specific pain entities such as **trigeminal nerve pain** and **facial nerve disorders** (including **facial paralysis** and upper‑facial atrophy) are linked to peripheral nerve pathology. Lesions of individual peripheral nerves—**plantar nerve**, **calcaneal (tibial) nerve**, **sciatic nerve**, **thoracic peripheral nerves**, and hemorrhage into a nerve trunk—are each associated with localized motor loss, foot deformities, or broader paralytic outcomes. **Peripheral neuropathy** is a central subcategory connected to pain and paralysis and subdivided into numerous etiologies: **alcoholic polyneuropathy**, **diabetic neuropathy**, **idiopathic progressive neuropathy**,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and the hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all clinical entities that have the nerve as their finding site, indicating that pathological changes in these conditions are localized to nerve tissue. Likewise, an incision of nerve is a direct procedural intervention performed on the nerve, underscoring the nerve as a common target for both disease processes and surgical treatment. Recognizing that the nerve serves as the anatomical locus for these diverse findings and procedures helps clinicians accurately diagnose neuropathic conditions, plan appropriate interventions, and coordinate patient care.

In addition, excision is employed in therapeutic procedures of the nose, mouth, and pharynx and is utilized in plastic surgery of the nose, illustrating its wide applicability across multiple organ systems within the hierarchical clinical‑action framework guiding diagnostic and therapeutic decision‑making.
